name: Room-Temperature Superconductors
what: Materials that would conduct electricity with zero resistance at everyday temperature and pressure — still unproven despite repeated claims.
how: Researchers search hydride and layered compounds computationally, then attempt synthesis under pressure and measure for a resistance drop.
use: Lossless power grids, magnets, maglev — if realised

name: Programmable Metamaterials
what: Engineered structures whose mechanical, acoustic or electromagnetic properties can be reconfigured on demand.
how: Sub-wavelength unit cells with embedded actuators or phase-change elements change shape or state to retune the bulk material response.

name: Self-Healing Composites
what: Polymers and composites that autonomously repair cracks and damage to extend service life.
how: Microcapsules or vascular networks release a healing agent into cracks, or reversible bonds re-form when heated.

name: Time Crystals
what: A phase of matter whose structure repeats in time rather than space, oscillating in a stable pattern without absorbing net energy.
how: A driven, many-body quantum system (trapped ions or qubits) is prepared so its collective state returns periodically at a fraction of the drive frequency.
use: Fundamental physics, potential quantum-memory and sensing research

name: MXenes (2D Transition-Metal Carbides)
what: A family of atomically thin, metallic-conducting 2D materials that are also readily processed in water, unusual for a conductor.
how: A precursor MAX phase is selectively etched to remove an atomic layer, leaving stackable nanosheets that can be coated, printed, or spun into films.
use: Electromagnetic shielding, flexible electrodes, energy storage
